pattern: Since an operation can only raise values, the cheapest fix is to raise each element to the smallest height that keeps it at least as tall as everything already fixed to its left, and the total cost of building that rising profile with range-add operations is just the sum of its upward jumps.
hints: An operation only ever adds a positive amount, so once an element is raised high enough to match the running maximum so far, there is never a reason to raise it any further than that.
Track a running maximum as you scan left to right; the minimal extra amount owed to each element is exactly that running maximum minus the element's own value (or zero if it is already tall enough).

Those per-element extra amounts form a profile that gets built up by range-add operations one contiguous layer at a time -- the total cost is the sum of every place that profile increases from the position before it, since a decrease is always free.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, two operations of size one each
statement example, a single operation covering three elements
a single element needs no operations at all
already non-decreasing, the answer is zero
strictly decreasing: every following element must be raised to match the running maximum
the required extra amounts are not monotonic, so a naive per-index difference (rather than a running-maximum delta) would undercount
a dip followed by a much bigger recovery so the running maximum jumps twice
values at the maximum bound to check the running maximum and cost sums stay exact at scale
all values equal, no operation is ever needed

stress.description: random arrays up to the full length constraint with values across the full range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 1000000000) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]

stress.description: long sawtooth arrays that repeatedly rise and fall, stressing the running-maximum bookkeeping
